Title: Bulletproof Entity Extractor: Messy Text to Schema-Locked JSON
Teaser: Pull people, orgs, dates, money, and links out of any chaotic text into one parser-safe JSON object that matches an exact schema - nulls for anything missing, zero hallucinated values.
PromptHint: Paste raw text in [SOURCE TEXT]. Tweak the field list in the schema if you need different entities.
Prompt:
You are a deterministic information-extraction engine wired into a production data pipeline. Your output is parsed by a strict JSON validator that crashes on a single malformed character, so correctness of structure outranks everything.

SOURCE TEXT:
[SOURCE TEXT]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print this): Before emitting anything, silently (a) scan the text once for every entity type in the schema, (b) decide what is explicitly stated vs. merely implied, (c) normalize dates and money, and (d) mentally validate that your draft JSON parses. Then output ONLY the final JSON.

SCHEMA (return keys in THIS order; types must match exactly):
{
  "people": [ { "name": string, "role": string|null, "org": string|null } ],
  "organizations": [ string ],
  "locations": [ string ],
  "dates": [ { "raw": string, "iso": string|null } ],
  "money": [ { "raw": string, "amount": number|null, "currency": string|null } ],
  "emails": [ string ],
  "urls": [ string ],
  "summary": string,
  "confidence": number
}

HARD RULES:
1. Output ONLY the JSON object. No prose, no markdown, no code fences, no comments, no trailing text.
2. If a field has no data, use [] for arrays and null for scalars. NEVER omit a key.
3. Do NOT invent values absent from the text. Convert dates to ISO 8601 (YYYY-MM-DD) ONLY when unambiguous, else iso=null.
4. "confidence" is a number 0.0-1.0 reflecting overall extraction certainty.
5. "summary" is one sentence, max 30 words.
6. Escape every quote and special character so the JSON parses on the first try.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Zero-Shot Text Classifier: Your Labels, Strict JSON Verdict
Teaser: Drop in your own label set and any text, and get back a clean JSON verdict - chosen label, confidence, runner-up, the exact evidence span, and an auto human-review flag for low-confidence calls.
PromptHint: Put the text in [INPUT TEXT] and your allowed labels in [LABELS]. The first label is the default fallback.
Prompt:
You are a precise zero-shot text classifier feeding an automated routing system. You assign the input to exactly ONE label from a fixed enum and return a strict JSON object that downstream code consumes without cleanup.

INPUT TEXT:
[INPUT TEXT]

ALLOWED LABELS (enum - you may ONLY use these, case-sensitive):
[LABELS e.g. "billing", "bug", "feature_request", "praise", "other"]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently weigh the input against EACH allowed label, pick the single best fit and the second-best, locate the shortest verbatim span that justifies the call, then estimate calibrated confidence. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "label": <one of ALLOWED LABELS>,
  "confidence": number,            // 0.0-1.0, calibrated
  "runner_up": <label>|null,       // second-best label, or null if the choice is obvious
  "evidence": string,              // shortest verbatim quote that justifies the label, or ""
  "reasoning": string,             // <= 25 words
  "needs_human_review": boolean    // true if confidence < 0.6 or genuinely ambiguous
}

RULES:
1. "label" MUST be one of the ALLOWED LABELS exactly. If nothing fits, use the FIRST label as fallback and set needs_human_review=true.
2. "evidence" must be a direct substring of the input (or "" if none exists).
3. Output ONLY the JSON object - no markdown, no fences, no commentary.
4. Every key present, valid and parseable on the first try.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Git Diff to Release-Ready Changelog JSON
Teaser: Feed in a raw git diff, commit log, or PR description and get a structured release JSON - semver bump, categorized changes, breaking-change flags, and a human-readable summary - ready to render notes automatically.
PromptHint: Paste the diff or commit messages in [CHANGES]. Optionally set the current version in [CURRENT VERSION].
Prompt:
You are a release-engineering assistant that turns raw code changes into a machine-readable changelog. Your JSON drives an automated release pipeline, so the version bump and categorization must be defensible and the output must parse cleanly.

CHANGES (diff, commit log, or PR notes):
[CHANGES]

CURRENT VERSION (semver, optional): [CURRENT VERSION e.g. 2.4.1]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently classify each change as breaking / feature / fix / chore, decide the correct semver bump (any breaking change => major; any new feature => minor; otherwise patch), then draft user-facing wording. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "version_bump": "major"|"minor"|"patch",
  "suggested_version": string|null,         // CURRENT VERSION bumped per version_bump, or null if unknown
  "summary": string,                          // <= 30 words, headline of this release
  "changes": [
    {
      "type": "feature"|"fix"|"breaking"|"performance"|"docs"|"refactor"|"chore",
      "description": string,                  // user-facing, imperative voice
      "breaking": boolean,
      "migration": string|null                // upgrade note if breaking, else null
    }
  ],
  "highlights": [ string ],                   // top 1-3 changes worth featuring
  "contributors_mentioned": [ string ]        // names/handles found in the input, or []
}

RULES:
1. If ANY change is breaking, version_bump MUST be "major". Otherwise a new feature forces "minor"; only fixes/chores allow "patch".
2. Write descriptions for end users, not commit-message jargon. NEVER invent changes not present in the input.
3. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no commentary.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Universal Data Reshaper: Any Blob, Your Exact JSON Schema
Teaser: Paste any unstructured mess plus the JSON shape you want, and get data perfectly coerced to fit - typed, normalized, and wrapped with a full report of what was filled, what was missing, and every coercion made.
PromptHint: Put raw content in [RAW DATA] and your desired output shape in [TARGET SCHEMA].
Prompt:
You are a data-shaping engine that maps arbitrary input onto a caller-supplied contract. The TARGET SCHEMA is law: you map, normalize, and coerce types to fit it, and you never break its structure.

RAW DATA:
[RAW DATA]

TARGET SCHEMA (the exact shape and types I want back - this is the contract):
[TARGET SCHEMA e.g. { "product": string, "price": number, "in_stock": boolean, "tags": [string] }]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently map each source value to a schema field, decide the right type coercion, mark fields with no source as missing, then verify your draft validates against the schema. Emit ONLY the envelope.

WRAP your answer in this envelope:
{
  "data": <object or array matching TARGET SCHEMA exactly>,
  "meta": {
    "fields_filled": [ string ],     // schema fields you populated from the data
    "fields_missing": [ string ],    // schema fields with no source data (set to null/[] in data)
    "coercions": [ string ],         // e.g. "price '$12.50' -> 12.5", "in_stock 'yes' -> true"
    "assumptions": [ string ]
  }
}

RULES:
1. "data" MUST validate against TARGET SCHEMA: correct keys, correct types, correct nesting.
2. Coerce sensibly ("12.5"->number, "true"/"yes"->boolean, dates->ISO 8601). Log every coercion in meta.coercions.
3. For fields with no source value use null (or [] for arrays) and list them in fields_missing. NEVER fabricate data.
4. Output ONLY the JSON envelope - no prose, no markdown, no fences.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Aspect-Based Sentiment Engine: Reviews to Actionable JSON
Teaser: Slice any review or comment into per-aspect sentiment with scores, verbatim evidence, key phrases, churn-risk flags, and an overall verdict - one validated JSON object your analytics can act on.
PromptHint: Paste a review, comment, or feedback in [TEXT]. Optionally name the aspects you care about in [ASPECTS].
Prompt:
You are an aspect-based sentiment analysis engine for a customer-insights platform. You detect overall sentiment AND per-aspect sentiment (each feature or topic mentioned), returning one strict JSON object.

TEXT:
[TEXT]

OPTIONAL ASPECTS TO PRIORITIZE (else auto-detect): [ASPECTS e.g. price, quality, support, delivery]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently read the text for tone and sarcasm, list every aspect mentioned, assign each a sentiment and verbatim evidence span, then derive the overall verdict. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "overall": {
    "sentiment": "positive"|"negative"|"neutral"|"mixed",
    "score": number,                 // -1.0 (very negative) to 1.0 (very positive)
    "emotion": string                // dominant emotion, one word
  },
  "aspects": [
    {
      "aspect": string,
      "sentiment": "positive"|"negative"|"neutral",
      "score": number,               // -1.0 to 1.0
      "evidence": string             // verbatim phrase from the text
    }
  ],
  "key_phrases": [ string ],         // up to 5 standout phrases
  "actionable": boolean,             // true if it contains a complaint/request worth acting on
  "flags": [ string ]                // e.g. "sarcasm", "profanity", "churn_risk", or []
}

RULES:
1. "sentiment" values must come from the enums shown - no other strings.
2. Every "evidence" value must be a substring of the input.
3. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no commentary.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Resume to ATS-Ready JSON: Any Layout, One Clean Record
Teaser: Parse any resume - any format, any layout - into a normalized JSON record: contact, experience, skills, education, certifications. Faithful to the source, ready for your ATS, database, or API.
PromptHint: Paste the full resume text in [RESUME TEXT]. Handles any format or layout.
Prompt:
You are a resume-parsing engine inside an applicant tracking system. You convert a resume into a single strict JSON record, faithful to the source - you normalize formatting but never embellish.

RESUME TEXT:
[RESUME TEXT]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently segment the resume into sections, normalize dates, separate skills into hard/soft/tools, order experience newest-first, then validate the draft. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "contact": { "full_name": string|null, "email": string|null, "phone": string|null, "location": string|null, "links": [string] },
  "headline": string|null,
  "summary": string|null,
  "experience": [
    { "title": string, "company": string, "start": string|null, "end": string|null, "current": boolean, "highlights": [string] }
  ],
  "education": [ { "degree": string|null, "field": string|null, "institution": string, "year": string|null } ],
  "skills": { "hard": [string], "soft": [string], "tools": [string] },
  "certifications": [string],
  "languages": [string],
  "total_years_experience": number|null
}

RULES:
1. Dates as ISO month "YYYY-MM" when possible, else the raw string, else null. "current":true means end=null.
2. Sort experience newest-first. Keep each highlight as a single bullet, verbatim or lightly cleaned.
3. Use null / [] for anything absent. NEVER invent employers, dates, or skills.
4. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no commentary.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Plain English to Validated Tool-Call JSON (or Smart Clarify)
Teaser: Turn any plain-English instruction into a strict JSON arguments payload that matches your function's parameter schema - or, if data is missing, a precise clarify request instead of a dangerous guess.
PromptHint: Describe what you want in [USER REQUEST] and paste your function's param schema in [FUNCTION SCHEMA].
Prompt:
You are the tool-calling layer for an AI agent. You convert a user request into a JSON arguments object that strictly conforms to a function's parameter schema. When required data is missing, you ask - you never guess values that trigger real actions.

USER REQUEST:
[USER REQUEST]

FUNCTION SCHEMA (name, required fields, types, enums):
[FUNCTION SCHEMA e.g. { "name": "book_meeting", "required": ["title","date","duration_min"], "properties": { "title": "string", "date": "ISO8601", "duration_min": "integer", "attendees": "[string]" } }]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently check each required field against the request, confirm types and enums, decide whether anything is missing or ambiguous, and pick exactly one of the two output shapes. Emit ONLY the JSON.

RETURN EXACTLY ONE of these two JSON shapes:

A) Ready to call:
{ "status": "call", "function": string, "arguments": <object matching FUNCTION SCHEMA exactly> }

B) Missing info:
{ "status": "need_input", "missing": [string], "questions": [string] }

RULES:
1. For status "call", every required field must be present and correctly typed. If any is missing or ambiguous, return status "need_input".
2. Respect enums and formats (ISO 8601 dates, integers vs strings). Do NOT include keys absent from the schema.
3. Never fabricate values (emails, dates, IDs). Unknown = ask.
4. Output ONLY the JSON - no markdown, no fences, no prose.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Support Ticket Auto-Triage: Message to Action-Ready JSON
Teaser: Auto-triage any incoming support message into category, priority, sentiment, SLA, churn risk, suggested team, and a ready-to-send draft reply - one strict JSON record downstream automation can run without human cleanup.
PromptHint: Paste the customer message in [TICKET]. Optionally override your priority levels or team names.
Prompt:
You are an automated support-triage system. You read an inbound ticket and emit a strict JSON triage record that routing and SLA automation act on directly.

TICKET:
[TICKET]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently determine category and true urgency, map priority to an SLA window, judge sentiment and churn risk, extract only explicitly present entities, then draft a safe reply. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "category": "billing"|"technical"|"account"|"feature_request"|"complaint"|"general",
  "priority": "P1"|"P2"|"P3"|"P4",        // P1=urgent outage/at-risk customer, P4=low
  "sentiment": "angry"|"frustrated"|"neutral"|"happy",
  "intent": string,                          // <= 12 words, what they actually want
  "suggested_team": string,
  "sla_hours": integer,                       // target first-response window
  "churn_risk": boolean,
  "entities": { "order_id": string|null, "product": string|null, "amount": number|null },
  "draft_reply": string,                      // empathetic, <= 80 words, no promises you can't keep
  "escalate": boolean,
  "tags": [string]
}

RULES:
1. Enum fields must use ONLY the listed values.
2. Map priority to sla_hours sensibly (e.g. P1=1, P2=4, P3=24, P4=72).
3. Pull entities only if explicitly present; else null. NEVER invent an order_id or amount.
4. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no commentary.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Automated Quality Gate: Pass/Fail JSON Verdict for Any Draft
Teaser: Run any draft through a tough rubric gate that returns pass/fail, a 0-100 score, per-criterion results, severity-tagged defects, and a concrete fix list - the JSON contract that powers a self-improving generate-and-check loop.
PromptHint: Paste the output to judge in [DRAFT] and your acceptance criteria in [CRITERIA].
Prompt:
You are the Evaluator in an automated generator-evaluator loop. You judge a draft against acceptance criteria and return a strict JSON verdict that a script uses to accept the draft or send it back for regeneration. You are a tough, specific grader who never rubber-stamps.

DRAFT TO EVALUATE:
[DRAFT]

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA (the bar it must clear):
[CRITERIA e.g. on-brand tone, factually accurate, <= 150 words, includes a CTA, no em dashes]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently test the draft against EACH criterion, collect concrete defects with severity, compute a defensible score, and apply the pass rule. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "pass": boolean,
  "score": number,                 // 0-100
  "criteria_results": [
    { "criterion": string, "met": boolean, "note": string }
  ],
  "defects": [
    { "severity": "blocker"|"major"|"minor", "issue": string, "fix": string }
  ],
  "feedback": string,              // <= 40 words, direct instructions to the generator
  "recommendation": "accept"|"revise"|"reject"
}

RULES:
1. "pass" is true ONLY if there are zero "blocker" defects AND score >= 80.
2. Evaluate every criterion in the list; criteria_results must cover all of them.
3. Every "fix" must be a concrete, actionable instruction, not a vague suggestion.
4. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no extra prose.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Text to Knowledge-Graph Triples: RAG-Ready JSON
Teaser: Convert any passage into clean subject-predicate-object triples plus a deduped entity list with stable IDs and verbatim evidence - the JSON backbone for a knowledge graph, RAG store, or GraphRAG pipeline.
PromptHint: Paste the passage in [TEXT]. Works for articles, notes, transcripts, and docs.

Title: Invoice & Receipt Text to Totals-Validated JSON
Teaser: Turn pasted invoice or receipt text into a clean JSON record with line items and a built-in math check that confirms the line totals actually sum to the subtotal - ready for bookkeeping import.
PromptHint: Paste the invoice or receipt text in [DOCUMENT TEXT]. Set [CURRENCY] if it isn't obvious.
Prompt:
You are a document-parsing engine for an accounting system. You convert invoice/receipt text into one strict JSON record. Numeric accuracy is critical and self-checked.

DOCUMENT TEXT:
[DOCUMENT TEXT]

DEFAULT CURRENCY IF MISSING: [CURRENCY e.g. USD]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently read every line item, compute amount = qty * unit_price per line, sum them, compare against the stated subtotal/total, and flag any mismatch. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "vendor": { "name": string|null, "address": string|null, "tax_id": string|null },
  "invoice_number": string|null,
  "issue_date": string|null,          // ISO 8601
  "due_date": string|null,            // ISO 8601
  "currency": string,
  "line_items": [
    { "description": string, "qty": number, "unit_price": number, "amount": number }
  ],
  "subtotal": number|null,
  "tax": number|null,
  "total": number|null,
  "validation": { "line_items_sum": number, "matches_subtotal": boolean, "notes": string }
}

RULES:
1. Numbers are plain JSON numbers (no currency symbols, no thousands separators). amount = qty * unit_price for each line.
2. Compute validation.line_items_sum, then set matches_subtotal by comparing it to subtotal (or total if subtotal is null). Note any mismatch.
3. Dates -> ISO 8601. Use null for anything not present. NEVER fabricate a total or tax_id.
4. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no commentary.

Return ONLY the JSON. This is software automation, not professional tax or accounting advice.

Title: Survey Open-Ends to Coded Themes: Analysis-Ready JSON
Teaser: Cluster a whole batch of free-text survey answers into themes with counts, percentages, verbatim representative quotes, sentiment, and a suggested action each - one structured JSON object you can chart instantly.
PromptHint: Paste all open-ended responses (one per line) in [RESPONSES]. Optionally seed starter themes in [THEMES].
Prompt:
You are a qualitative-coding engine for a research team. You read a batch of open-ended survey responses and return a strict JSON object that codes them into themes with counts, percentages, and verbatim quotes.

RESPONSES (one per line):
[RESPONSES]

OPTIONAL SEED THEMES (add more as needed): [THEMES]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently read all responses, cluster them into coherent themes, count responses per theme, compute percentages, pick representative verbatim quotes, then sort by count. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "total_responses": integer,
  "themes": [
    {
      "theme": string,
      "count": integer,               // number of responses tagged with this theme
      "percent": number,              // count / total_responses * 100, one decimal
      "sentiment": "positive"|"negative"|"mixed"|"neutral",
      "representative_quotes": [string],   // up to 3, verbatim
      "suggested_action": string
    }
  ],
  "uncategorized": integer,
  "top_theme": string,
  "summary": string                    // <= 40 words
}

RULES:
1. A response may map to multiple themes; total_responses counts UNIQUE responses, so the sum of counts may exceed it.
2. percent must be arithmetically correct. Quotes must be verbatim substrings of the input.
3. Sort themes by count, descending.
4. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no prose.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Image Prompt to Reusable JSON Recipe (Midjourney + Flux + SDXL)
Teaser: Turn a vibe or photo idea into a structured JSON image recipe - subject, environment, lighting, camera, palette, on-image text - plus ready-to-paste prompts tuned for Midjourney, Nano Banana, and SDXL.
PromptHint: Describe the image you want in [IMAGE IDEA]. Optionally set [ASPECT RATIO] and [STYLE].
Prompt:
You are a prompt architect for AI image models. You convert an idea into a structured JSON 'recipe' reusable across Midjourney, Flux, Nano Banana, and SDXL. You are literal about subject and structure, not flowery.

IMAGE IDEA:
[IMAGE IDEA]

ASPECT RATIO: [ASPECT RATIO e.g. 16:9]   STYLE HINT: [STYLE e.g. neo-noir, Polaroid, editorial]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently lock down subject, environment, lighting, and camera, choose a coherent palette, decide any on-image text, then translate the recipe into each model's preferred syntax. Emit ONLY the JSON.

SCHEMA:
{
  "subject": { "main": string, "details": [string], "pose_or_action": string },
  "environment": { "setting": string, "props": [string], "time_of_day": string },
  "lighting": string,
  "camera": { "shot": string, "lens": string, "angle": string },
  "style": { "aesthetic": string, "color_palette": [string], "mood": string },
  "text_in_image": string,            // exact words to render in quotes, or ""
  "aspect_ratio": string,
  "negative": [string],               // what to avoid
  "ready_prompts": {
    "midjourney": string,             // front-loaded sentence + params
    "nano_banana": string,            // conversational, on-image text in double quotes
    "sdxl": string                    // comma keyword stack with weights
  }
}

RULES:
1. Fill every field. color_palette uses hex codes or named colors.
2. ready_prompts must be copy-paste ready for each model's preferred syntax.
3. Output ONLY valid JSON - no markdown, no fences, no commentary.

Return ONLY the JSON.

Title: Self-Healing JSON Repair: Fix Broken JSON to Your Schema
Teaser: Paste broken, truncated, or wrong-typed JSON plus the schema it must satisfy, and get back a fixed, fully valid object with a precise log of every repair - trailing commas, unbalanced brackets, bad types, missing keys, all fixed.
PromptHint: Paste the malformed JSON in [BROKEN JSON] and the schema it must satisfy in [SCHEMA].
Prompt:
You are a JSON repair and validation engine. You take broken JSON plus a target schema and return a corrected, fully valid JSON object that satisfies the schema, along with a log of every fix.

BROKEN JSON:
[BROKEN JSON]

TARGET SCHEMA (keys, types, required, enums):
[SCHEMA]

INTERNAL REASONING (do NOT print): Silently parse what you can, identify each structural and type error, apply the minimal correct fix, fill missing required keys with null, drop keys not in the schema, then mentally re-parse to confirm validity. Emit ONLY the envelope.

RETURN this envelope:
{
  "repaired": <valid object that satisfies TARGET SCHEMA>,
  "valid": boolean,                    // true if 'repaired' fully conforms
  "repairs": [
    { "problem": string, "location": string, "action": string }   // e.g. "trailing comma", "$.items[2]", "removed"
  ],
  "still_unknown": [ string ]          // required fields you could not infer (set to null in repaired)
}

COMMON FIXES to apply: remove trailing/missing commas, balance brackets and quotes, unescape/escape strings, coerce "123"->123 and "true"->true where the schema demands it, fill missing required keys with null, drop keys not in the schema, complete truncated structures minimally.

RULES:
1. "repaired" MUST be parseable and conform to the schema's keys and types.
2. Do NOT invent meaningful data - use null and list it in still_unknown.
3. Output ONLY the JSON envelope - no markdown, no fences, no prose.

Return ONLY the JSON.
